subtopic_detail: Roseola - recognition of post-defervescence rash pattern
stem: A 14-month-old fully immunised boy is reviewed in suburban general practice after three days of high fever to 39.7 deg C that resolved overnight. He is now afebrile and playful with a blanching, maculopapular, trunk-predominant rash that appeared this morning, sparing face, palms and soles. He has had no cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, oral lesions, or diarrhoea. There is no lymphadenopathy and no meningism, capillary refill is under two seconds, and mucous membranes are moist. He attends family daycare, where a 4-year-old sibling of another child has confirmed measles diagnosed yesterday by the public health unit and other children with similar fever-then-rash presentations have been seen at this practice in the past fortnight. The MMR vaccine is due at 18 months and has not yet been given. There is no household member who is immunocompromised or pregnant. The mother asks about diagnosis, time off daycare, and whether her son needs anything more.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C (previously 39.7), HR 118, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Blanching pink maculopapular rash on trunk/neck, sparing face/palms/soles; no Koplik spots, no conjunctivitis, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Immunisations: Up to date for age (MMR not yet due)

lead_in: Which combination of diagnosis and exclusion advice is most appropriate?

options.text: Roseola infantum; he may return to family daycare once afebrile and well, with no formal childcare exclusion period required under Australian guidance
options.rationale: Fever-then-rash in a well 6 to 24-month-old (trunk-predominant blanching rash after defervescence) is classical roseola (HHV-6). Under NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH guidance, roseola does not require formal childcare exclusion; exclusion ends once the child is afebrile and well, per NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

options.text: Measles given the daycare contact; exclude from family daycare for 4 days from rash onset and notify the public health unit
options.rationale: Measles requires fever PLUS cough, coryza or conjunctivitis with a cephalocaudal rash, none of which are present here; the well, post-defervescence trunk-predominant rash is roseola. Notification is required for measles but is not appropriate here, per NCIRS: Measles fact sheet and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

options.text: Rubella; exclude from family daycare for 7 days from rash onset and arrange confirmatory serology before any return
options.rationale: Rubella shows tender suboccipital and post-auricular lymphadenopathy with a face-first cephalocaudal rash, not the trunk-predominant post-defervescence pattern seen here, per RCH CPG: Rubella.

options.text: Scarlet fever; commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 10 days and exclude from daycare for 24 hours after starting antibiotics
options.rationale: Scarlet fever has a sandpaper-textured rash with strawberry tongue, exudative pharyngitis and circumoral pallor; none are present and antibiotics are not indicated, per eTG: Group A streptococcal infection.

options.text: Roseola infantum; commence oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for 5 days and exclude from daycare for the duration of treatment
options.rationale: Roseola is self-limiting; antivirals are not indicated in immunocompetent infants, and there is no daycare-exclusion requirement beyond being afebrile and well, per RCH CPG: Viral exanthems and NHMRC Staying Healthy.

explanation.summary: Roseola (HHV-6) classically causes 3 to 5 days of high fever in a well-appearing 6 to 24-month-old, with a blanching trunk-predominant rash erupting AS the fever resolves (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
key_points: Fever-then-rash pattern is the defining clinical clue (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
Self-limiting; no specific treatment beyond antipyresis and reassurance (StatPearls: Roseola).
Febrile convulsions occur in around 10 to 15% of cases during the fever phase (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
explanation.pearls: If a well child has a rash AS the fever breaks, think roseola; if the rash appears DURING fever with cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, think measles.

subtopic_detail: Measles - prodrome and Koplik spots before rash onset
stem: A 3-year-old unimmunised boy is brought to a regional Victorian emergency department with four days of fever to 39.8 deg C, barking moist cough, profuse coryza, and red watery photophobic eyes. He returned 10 days ago from a 3-week family trip to a rural province in Vietnam. He has no skin rash yet. On examination he is miserable with bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, clear nasal discharge, and several 1 to 2 mm bluish-white papules on an erythematous base on the buccal mucosa opposite the second molars. Temperature 39.6 deg C, HR 138, RR 32, SpO2 97 percent room air. He has no rash, no lymphadenopathy, chest is clear, and he is haemodynamically stable. He is currently in an open paediatric ED bay alongside other waiting children, including a 6-month-old infant and a pregnant accompanying parent. You strongly suspect measles based on Koplik spots and the three Cs.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 138, RR 32, SpO2 97% RA, BP 95/55
data_table.Examination: Bilateral conjunctivitis, coryza, productive cough, Koplik spots on buccal mucosa; no skin rash yet
data_table.History: Unimmunised; recent travel from Vietnam 10 days ago

lead_in: Which next step is most appropriate before laboratory confirmation?

options.text: Move him to a negative-pressure single room with airborne and contact precautions, and phone the public health unit to begin notification now
options.rationale: Measles is a notifiable disease in all Australian jurisdictions and clinical suspicion (especially with Koplik spots and an epidemiological link) triggers immediate isolation in a negative-pressure room with airborne and contact precautions and same-day phone notification to the public health unit BEFORE laboratory confirmation, to enable rapid contact tracing and PEP within the windows (MMR within 72 h, NHIG within 7 days), per CDNA: Measles SoNG and NCIRS: Measles fact sheet.

options.text: Send measles IgM and PCR (throat swab and urine) and await laboratory confirmation before notifying public health or isolating
options.rationale: Waiting for laboratory confirmation before isolation and notification breaches Australian public health practice and delays contact tracing and post-exposure prophylaxis for susceptible contacts (the infant and pregnant parent in the bay), per CDNA: Measles SoNG.

options.text: Place him in a standard single room with droplet precautions only and await senior review before any notification action
options.rationale: Measles is airborne; droplet precautions alone are inadequate and a negative-pressure room with N95 respirator entry is required, per CDNA: Measles SoNG.

options.text: Administer stat MMR vaccine and oral vitamin A 200 000 IU now, then discharge with isolation advice and review tomorrow
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in active suspected measles infection; vitamin A is recommended in confirmed cases of severe measles but does not replace isolation, notification, and inpatient assessment, and discharge before notification is unsafe, per WHO Measles guidelines and CDNA: Measles SoNG.

options.text: Commence empirical IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg and admit for full sepsis workup including lumbar puncture before any isolation measures
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classical measles, not bacterial sepsis or meningitis; antibiotics and LP are not indicated and isolation plus notification take priority, per RCH CPG: Febrile child and CDNA: Measles SoNG.

explanation.summary: Koplik spots, found on the buccal mucosa 1 to 2 days before rash onset, are pathognomonic for measles and allow earlier diagnosis and public health action before the typical caudal-spreading exanthem appears (NCIRS: Measles fact sheet).
key_points: Measles prodrome: fever + cough + coryza + conjunctivitis (the three Cs) (NCIRS: Measles).
Koplik spots appear 1 to 2 days BEFORE the rash (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
Rash starts at the hairline/face and spreads caudally over 3 days (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
Notifiable disease - urgent public health notification required at clinical suspicion (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
explanation.pearls: Suspect-then-isolate-then-test: place the child in a negative pressure single room with airborne and contact precautions and phone the public health unit before laboratory confirmation.

subtopic_detail: Measles - public health management and contact prophylaxis
stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised child has measles confirmed on PCR after a maculopapular rash spread from the face caudally over 24 hours. He had three days of fever, cough, coryza, and conjunctivitis before the rash, and Koplik spots were identified at the GP visit the day before rash onset. He attended a community playgroup on the day before his rash appeared and the morning of rash onset, while infectious. The public health unit has been notified and is contacting the playgroup register. Among the playgroup contacts identified within the 4 hours before to 4 days after his rash, several need urgent post-exposure management decisions. They include: a 5-month-old infant whose mother is rubella-immune but has no documented measles history or serology; a 13-month-old child who received a single dose of MMR five days ago; a 35-year-old pregnant playgroup helper at 22 weeks gestation with no documented measles immunity and a negative measles IgG on urgent serology done this morning; a 7-year-old child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukaemia in week 2 of induction chemotherapy and currently neutropenic; and a 30-year-old playgroup leader born in 1992 with two documented MMR doses on her workplace immunisation record.

data_table.Exposure window: 4 hours before prodrome to 4 days after rash onset (still infectious)
data_table.Contacts requiring decision: 5-month infant; 13-month with one MMR five days ago; pregnant 22-week non-immune helper; 7-year-old on induction chemo for ALL; 30-year-old with 2x MMR documented
data_table.Time since exposure: Within 72 hours for all listed contacts

lead_in: Which contact most appropriately receives normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) rather than MMR vaccine as post-exposure prophylaxis?

options.text: The 5-month-old infant, given MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is not given under 6 months (poorly immunogenic due to maternal antibody); infants under 6 months exposed to measles should receive NHIG, but the option as written specifies MMR which is wrong. Would be correct if NHIG were specified for the 5-month-old (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The 13-month-old who received MMR five days ago, given a second MMR now
options.rationale: MMR five days before exposure is too recent for protective immunity but a repeat dose within 72 h may still help; NHIG is not first-line here. Would be correct as repeat MMR for that contact (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The pregnant 22-week non-immune playgroup helper, given NHIG within 6 days of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is a live vaccine and CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy. A non-immune pregnant contact within 6 days of exposure should receive NHIG to attenuate or prevent maternal measles and complications; serology has confirmed she is non-immune (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles; CDNA: Measles SoNG).

options.text: The 7-year-old with ALL on induction chemotherapy, given MMR within 72 hours of exposure
options.rationale: MMR is contraindicated in severely immunocompromised including those on induction chemo; this child should receive NHIG. Option as written is incorrect. Would be correct if NHIG were specified (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: The 30-year-old playgroup leader with two documented MMR doses, given a third MMR booster
options.rationale: Two documented MMR doses confer presumptive immunity; no further intervention/booster required. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

explanation.summary: For susceptible measles contacts, MMR within 72 hours is preferred in those eligible for live vaccine, but NHIG within 6 days is indicated in those for whom live vaccine is contraindicated: infants under 6 months, pregnant non-immune contacts, and severely immunocompromised patients (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Measles).
key_points: MMR within 72h of exposure for eligible non-immune contacts (AIH: Measles).
NHIG within 6 days for pregnant non-immune, infants <6 months, severely immunocompromised (AIH: Measles).
Two documented MMR doses count as presumptive immunity in Australia (AIH: Measles).
Measles is notifiable - phone the public health unit on clinical suspicion (CDNA: Measles SoNG).
explanation.pearls: Pregnant non-immune contacts get NHIG, never MMR - live vaccine is contraindicated in pregnancy.

stem: A 6-year-old boy is in week 3 of induction chemotherapy for high-risk B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia at a tertiary paediatric oncology centre. He shared a 1-hour indoor classroom session in the day hospital school room with another child who has since been confirmed by PCR to have early varicella, with a vesicular rash that had been present for less than 24 hours during the session. The exposure was 36 hours ago. The boy has had no past episode of clinical varicella, no varicella vaccination on his record (his older sibling refused MMRV at 18 months for the family on philosophical grounds and the patient was diagnosed before catch-up), and his pre-treatment baseline varicella IgG was negative on serology drawn before induction. He is currently profoundly neutropenic with an absolute neutrophil count of 0.4 x 10^9/L, has no rash, no fever, no oral lesions, and feels well.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 96, RR 20, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Well; no rash, no vesicles, no oral lesions
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 0.4 x 10^9/L, pre-chemo varicella IgG negative
data_table.History: ALL induction week 3; significant face-to-face exposure 36 hours ago to a child with early varicella

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer the varicella vaccine within 72 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Varicella vaccine is live attenuated and CONTRAINDICATED in severely immunocompromised including those on induction chemo. Would be correct only in immunocompetent contacts (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Administer zoster immunoglobulin (ZIG/VZIG) intramuscularly within 96 hours of exposure as post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severely immunocompromised susceptible contacts (varicella IgG negative, no prior infection or vaccination) with a significant exposure should receive ZIG within 96 hours (preferably as soon as possible, but the AIH allows extension up to 10 days in some circumstances) to attenuate or prevent disseminated varicella (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).

options.text: Commence prophylactic oral valaciclovir from day 7 to day 21 post-exposure
options.rationale: Antiviral prophylaxis (days 7-14) is adjunct or alternative when ZIG unavailable; ZIG is preferred first-line. Would be correct when ZIG unavailable (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Reassurance and observation, treating with intravenous aciclovir only if a rash develops
options.rationale: Waiting for the rash forgoes the proven benefit of ZIG given before viraemia; disseminated varicella in immunocompromised carries significant mortality. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Send urgent varicella IgG and IgM and decide on prophylaxis based on the result
options.rationale: Pre-treatment varicella IgG was negative documenting susceptibility; waiting for repeat serology delays ZIG and reduces effectiveness. Would never be correct (Source: Australian Immunisation Handbook).

explanation.summary: Severely immunocompromised susceptible children with significant varicella exposure should receive ZIG (zoster immunoglobulin) as soon as possible, ideally within 96 hours; live varicella vaccine is contraindicated in this group (Australian Immunisation Handbook: Varicella).
key_points: Significant exposure = household OR >=1 hour indoor face-to-face OR shared room OR substantial hospital contact (AIH: Varicella).
Susceptible = no past disease, no vaccination, and seronegative if tested (AIH: Varicella).
ZIG first-line in immunocompromised, neonates, and non-immune pregnant women (AIH: Varicella).
Antiviral prophylaxis day 7 to 14 is an adjunct/alternative when ZIG unavailable (AIH: Varicella).
explanation.pearls: Neonates born to mothers who develop varicella from 7 days before to 2 days after delivery also need ZIG - very high mortality without it.

subtopic_detail: Meningococcal disease - non-blanching rash recognition vs HSP
stem: A 2-year-old fully immunised girl is brought to a regional emergency department by ambulance after her parents noticed a rapidly spreading rash. Six hours ago she became unwell with fever and irritability; she has now had two episodes of non-bilious vomiting and is increasingly drowsy and floppy. On examination she is grey, mottled, and lethargic with poor central tone. Temperature 38.9 deg C, heart rate 168, respiratory rate 44, SpO2 96% on room air, blood pressure 78/40, capillary refill 5 seconds peripherally and 4 seconds centrally. There are scattered non-blanching petechiae and several larger irregular purpuric lesions on the trunk, abdomen and proximal limbs that have spread visibly while in the resuscitation cubicle over the last 10 minutes. Her neck is supple but she cries when picked up and her anterior fontanelle is no longer palpable. No focal neurology. She has had no recent URTI, no arthritis, no abdominal distension, no rash above the waist before today. Her routine immunisations including MenB and MenACWY are up to date as confirmed on the Australian Immunisation Register printout her parents have brought.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 deg C, HR 168, RR 44, SpO2 96% RA, BP 78/40, CRT 5 sec
data_table.Examination: Grey, mottled, drowsy; spreading non-blanching purpuric rash on trunk and proximal limbs
data_table.History: 6h illness; fully immunised including MenB and MenACWY

lead_in: Which is the single most important immediate intervention?

options.text: Obtain IV or IO access and give IV ceftriaxone without delay
options.rationale: This is meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise - rapidly spreading non-blanching rash with shock. Empirical IV (or IO if access difficult) ceftriaxone should be given as soon as access is obtained, ideally after cultures but NEVER delayed for them; fluid resuscitation, ABC support, and ICU/retrieval activation run in parallel (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection; ANZCOR/APLS).

options.text: Arrange urgent CT head and then lumbar puncture given the drowsiness and rash
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated in haemodynamic instability, coagulopathy, or rapidly evolving purpura; CT-then-LP delays the only intervention that changes outcome. Would be correct only in stable patient with normal coags (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Defer antibiotics until blood cultures are processed to maximise diagnostic yield from cultures
options.rationale: Delaying antibiotics in suspected meningococcal sepsis worsens mortality; cultures drawn only if access rapid, antibiotics must NOT wait. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Commence IV hydrocortisone before antibiotics to pre-treat Waterhouse-Friderichsen adrenal failure
options.rationale: Routine steroids are not the immediate priority and do not replace urgent antibiotics; stress-dose steroids may be considered later for refractory shock. Would be correct only later as adjunct in refractory shock (Source: ANZICS).

options.text: Reassess in 30 minutes after antipyretics as her current immunisations make invasive bacterial illness unlikely
options.rationale: Even fully immunised children can develop IMD from non-vaccine strains or vaccine failure; the picture mandates immediate empirical therapy. Would never be correct (Source: CDNA IMD SoNG).

explanation.summary: Suspected meningococcal septicaemia mandates immediate IV/IO ceftriaxone alongside fluid resuscitation - antibiotics must not be delayed by imaging or laboratory workup. Cultures are taken if access is fast; otherwise antibiotics go first (RCH CPG: Meningococcal infection).
key_points: Spreading non-blanching rash + shock = meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise (RCH: Meningococcal infection).
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) IV/IO without delay (eTG: Meningococcal disease).
Activate paediatric retrieval (NETS or state equivalent) early (RCH: Meningococcal infection).
Antibiotic prophylaxis for household and intimate contacts via public health unit (CDNA: Meningococcal SoNG).
explanation.pearls: If the petechiae are spreading WHILE you watch, you have minutes - antibiotics now, lines + fluids in parallel, retrieval call goes in before the third bolus.

subtopic_detail: Scarlet fever - recognition and antibiotic selection
stem: A 7-year-old previously well Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed during a paediatric outreach clinic in her community with a 2-day history of fever, sore throat and a rash that began today on the neck and chest before spreading to the trunk. She has no cough, coryza, or conjunctivitis. Temperature 38.6 deg C, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99 percent room air, capillary refill under two seconds. On examination she has a fine, blanching, erythematous papular eruption with a sandpaper texture accentuated in the antecubital and inguinal flexures, prominent fungiform papillae against a coated tongue, exudative tonsillitis, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and circumoral pallor. She has no known drug allergies, is fully immunised, has no history of acute rheumatic fever, and the family confirms she will be staying in community for at least the next two weeks. Throat swab for group A streptococcus has been collected. There is no current penicillin shortage in the community pharmacy.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 110, RR 22, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Sandpaper rash with flexural accentuation, strawberry tongue, exudative tonsillitis, tender cervical nodes, circumoral pallor
data_table.History: No allergies; non-remote setting; fully immunised

lead_in: Which first-line antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg (up to 500 mg) twice daily for 10 days, with community follow-up at completion
options.rationale: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin 10 days is first-line in non-high-ARF-risk Australian settings, but in high-ARF-risk settings (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in remote/regional areas, Maori and Pacific children) the preferred regimen is a single dose of IM benzathine benzylpenicillin to ensure compliance and prevent acute rheumatic fever, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline and eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

options.text: Single IM benzathine benzylpenicillin (900 mg if 20 kg or more, 450 mg if under 20 kg) administered now
options.rationale: In an Aboriginal child from a high-ARF-risk remote setting with clinical scarlet fever (group A streptococcal pharyngitis with toxin-mediated exanthem), a single dose of IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is the recommended Australian regimen to guarantee adherence and reduce ARF and post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis risk, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline and eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 50 mg/kg once daily for 10 days as a more palatable equivalent to first-line oral penicillin
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is acceptable where penicillin V is unpalatable, but in high-ARF-risk settings an IM regimen is preferred to remove adherence as a failure mode, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

options.text: Oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg (up to 1 g) twice daily for 10 days as initial therapy
options.rationale: Cefalexin is broader-spectrum than necessary and reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy; it is not first-line in either standard or high-ARF-risk Australian settings, per eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis.

options.text: Symptomatic care with paracetamol and reassurance, with antibiotics only if the throat swab returns positive in 48 hours
options.rationale: Clinical scarlet fever with high ARF risk warrants immediate empirical antibiotics; awaiting culture results delays prevention of ARF and is not consistent with Australian guidance, per RHDAustralia ARF Guideline.

explanation.summary: Scarlet fever is group A streptococcal pharyngitis plus a toxin-mediated sandpaper-textured rash with strawberry tongue. First-line in Australia is phenoxymethylpenicillin 15 mg/kg (max 500 mg) BD for 10 days (eTG: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis).
key_points: Sandpaper rash + strawberry tongue + flexural accentuation + circumoral pallor = scarlet fever (RCH: GAS).
First-line: penicillin V 10 days (eTG).
Severe penicillin allergy: macrolide (eTG).
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children in high-ARF-risk settings have a different regimen and stricter follow-up (RHDAustralia).
explanation.pearls: Desquamation of fingertips and palms 1 to 2 weeks after the rash is a late clue if you missed the initial illness.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Group A streptococcal disease

subtopic_detail: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease - recognition and discrimination from HSV
stem: A 22-month-old fully immunised girl is reviewed in general practice with three days of low-grade fever to 38.4 deg C, drooling, and reduced oral intake. Her mother noticed today that she has spots on her hands, feet, and inside her mouth. On examination she is alert and well-perfused. Temperature 37.6 deg C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent room air. There are shallow ulcers on the tongue, buccal mucosa, and posterior pharynx, and several small grey-roofed oval vesicles on an erythematous base on the palms, soles, and dorsa of the hands and feet. There is no lip clustering, no gingival inflammation, and no lymphadenopathy. The nappy area shows a few similar vesicles. Mucous membranes are slightly dry but she has just taken 50 mL of cold water in clinic. There is a known outbreak of vesicular illness at her childcare centre that includes one recent admission to the local children's hospital. You diagnose hand-foot-and-mouth disease. Her mother asks what to do about childcare attendance and what warning signs would warrant urgent return.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 deg C, HR 116, RR 28, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Oval grey-roofed vesicles on palms/soles/dorsa of hands and feet, shallow oral ulcers including posterior pharynx, sparse nappy-area lesions
data_table.History: Childcare outbreak; fully immunised; no prior cold sores

lead_in: Which combination of childcare exclusion advice and red-flag escalation criteria is most appropriate?

options.text: Exclude from childcare until all blisters have dried; return urgently for vomiting, ataxia, myoclonus, or reduced wet nappies
options.rationale: Australian guidance (NHMRC Staying Healthy, RCH) recommends childcare exclusion until all blisters have dried (not just until afebrile). Red flags for severe enterovirus disease (especially EV71) include neurological signs (ataxia, myoclonus, irritability), persistent high fever beyond 3 days, persistent vomiting, and inability to maintain hydration, all of which warrant urgent reassessment, per NHMRC Staying Healthy and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

options.text: Exclude from childcare only until afebrile for 24 hours, then return regardless of any remaining skin lesions
options.rationale: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease requires exclusion until blisters have dried under NHMRC Staying Healthy, not merely until afebrile; the lesions remain shedding virus, per NHMRC Staying Healthy.

options.text: No childcare exclusion required as HFMD is self-limiting; return whenever the child is willing and able
options.rationale: Australian public health guidance does specify formal exclusion until blisters dry for hand-foot-and-mouth disease; no-exclusion advice is incorrect, per NHMRC Staying Healthy.

options.text: Commence oral aciclovir 20 mg/kg four times daily for 5 days and exclude from childcare for the duration of treatment
options.rationale: Aciclovir has no role in enteroviral HFMD; the diagnosis is not herpes simplex and antivirals are not indicated, per eTG: Herpes simplex and RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

options.text: Admit now for IV fluids and serial neurological observations, given the childcare outbreak link to EV71
options.rationale: She is clinically well, alert, perfused, and tolerating oral fluids in clinic; routine admission is not required and supportive outpatient care with explicit red-flag advice is appropriate, per RCH CPG: Viral exanthems.

explanation.summary: Hand-foot-and-mouth disease is an enteroviral infection (often coxsackie A16, less commonly EV71) causing low-grade fever, posterior-predominant oral ulcers and oval vesicles on palms, soles, and dorsa of hands/feet, often with nappy-area involvement in toddlers (RCH CPG: Viral exanthems).
key_points: Self-limiting; supportive care with hydration and analgesia (RCH: Viral exanthems).
Childcare exclusion: until all blisters have dried (NHMRC Staying Healthy).
EV71 strain can cause severe complications including aseptic meningitis, brainstem encephalitis, and acute flaccid paralysis (StatPearls: HFMD).
Differentiate from HSV gingivostomatitis: HSV = anterior/gingival, ill child, no acral lesions; HFMD = posterior oropharynx, acral lesions (RCH: Viral exanthems).
explanation.pearls: If a toddler refuses to drink because of mouth pain, accept dehydration is the real risk and admit for IV fluids - it is the only common reason HFMD comes back to ED.

subtopic_detail: Pregnant contact of parvovirus B19 - obstetric implications
stem: A 32-year-old early childhood teacher at 18 weeks of her second pregnancy attends her GP after multiple confirmed cases of erythema infectiosum (slapped cheek) in the kindergarten class she teaches over the past fortnight. She had close prolonged indoor contact with two affected children one week ago, before they were excluded with their characteristic facial erythema and lacy peripheral rash. She is asymptomatic with no rash, arthralgia or fever. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated and her 4-year-old is in her class. Her 18-week morphology scan last week showed structurally normal anatomy with appropriate growth and amniotic fluid volume, and her first-trimester combined screen was low risk. She has no documented past clinical history of slapped cheek and no parvovirus B19 serology on file. Vital signs are normal, fundal height is consistent with dates, and her physical examination is unremarkable. She specifically asks whether she needs immediate fetal medicine referral or whether further blood tests come first.

data_table.Examination: Normal, no rash, gravid uterus at fundal height consistent with dates
data_table.History: 18 weeks pregnant; significant occupational exposure to confirmed parvovirus B19 1 week ago; no documented prior serology

lead_in: Which next investigation or referral is most appropriate?

options.text: Maternal parvovirus B19 IgG and IgM serology now, with further action determined by the IgG and IgM pattern
options.rationale: Australian (RANZCOG) and KEMH guidance: serology first is the AU-recommended pathway. IgG positive / IgM negative confirms pre-existing immunity (around 50 percent of women) and no further action is needed. IgG and IgM both negative means susceptible: repeat serology in 4 weeks. IgM positive (or IgG seroconversion) is confirmed recent infection and triggers MFM referral for serial fetal ultrasound and middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler velocimetry for at least 8 to 12 weeks, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy and KEMH: Parvovirus B19.

options.text: Immediate referral to maternal fetal medicine for weekly MCA Doppler velocimetry from now for 12 weeks regardless of serology
options.rationale: MCA Doppler surveillance is the correct AFTER-confirmation step but is unnecessary if she is already immune (around 50 percent prevalence) or if serology shows no infection; routine empirical Doppler before serology overcommits resources and is not the AU-recommended pathway, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

options.text: Urgent amniocentesis with parvovirus B19 PCR on amniotic fluid now to confirm or exclude vertical fetal transmission
options.rationale: Amniocentesis is reserved for confirmed maternal infection with evidence of fetal compromise; it is invasive and inappropriate as a first-line investigation in an asymptomatic exposed contact, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

options.text: Intramuscular normal human immunoglobulin (NHIG) administered within 7 days of exposure as parvovirus post-exposure prophylaxis
options.rationale: NHIG is not indicated for parvovirus B19 exposure in pregnancy; there is no proven prophylactic benefit and current Australian guidance recommends serology and surveillance instead, per Australian Immunisation Handbook.

options.text: Reassure and discharge as parvovirus B19 has no documented effect on a pregnancy past 12 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Maternal parvovirus B19 infection can cause fetal anaemia and hydrops fetalis with the highest risk in the second trimester (around 9 to 20 weeks); discharge without serology at 18 weeks is unsafe, per RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy.

explanation.summary: Pregnant women exposed to parvovirus B19 need serology to determine immune status; confirmed acute maternal infection in early-to-mid pregnancy triggers MFM-led MCA Doppler surveillance for foetal anaemia and hydrops (RANZCOG: Parvovirus in pregnancy).
key_points: About 50% of women of reproductive age are already immune (RANZCOG).
IgG positive / IgM negative = immune, reassure (RANZCOG).
IgG and IgM both negative = susceptible, repeat in 4 weeks (RANZCOG).
IgM positive = confirmed recent infection, refer MFM for serial MCA Doppler and ultrasound for hydrops (RANZCOG).
explanation.pearls: Highest risk for foetal loss/hydrops is exposure before 20 weeks; after 20 weeks the foetus has greater haematopoietic reserve and the foetal risk drops sharply.

subtopic_detail: Tinea corporis vs pityriasis rosea - discrimination and management
stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to general practice with a 10-day history of an enlarging itchy rash on the right side of his trunk. It began as a single oval pink scaly patch about 4 cm across, with a slightly raised edge and clearing centre. Over the last week several smaller similar pink scaly patches have appeared on his back, both flanks, and proximal arms. He plays competitive judo and shares mats and rashguards weekly with several teammates. One teammate has had a similar-looking rash on the arm for several weeks. He is otherwise well, afebrile, and has no preceding URTI symptoms. There is no mucosal involvement. Examination shows scaly annular lesions with active erythematous scaly borders and central clearing; the original 'herald' lesion is the largest. There is no Christmas-tree distribution along the lines of Langer, no oral ulceration. Direct mycological microscopy of skin scrapings from a border lesion is positive for septate hyphae.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 86, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA
data_table.Examination: Multiple annular scaly lesions with raised active scaly border and central clearing on trunk and proximal arms; largest 'herald' lesion 4 cm
data_table.Investigations: Skin scraping microscopy: septate hyphae present
data_table.History: Shared judo mats; teammate with similar rash

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Reassurance, emollient, and observation for 6 to 8 weeks for self-resolution
options.rationale: Pityriasis rosea management; mycology positive here excludes pityriasis. Would be correct only with mycology-negative herald-patch-Christmas-tree pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Topical terbinafine 1% cream twice daily for 2 weeks beyond the visible border
options.rationale: Microscopy confirms septate hyphae - this is tinea corporis. Topical terbinafine 1% twice daily for 1 to 2 weeks (continuing for 1 week after clinical resolution) is first-line for limited disease, with lesion-edge application extending beyond the visible border (eTG: Tinea; RCH CPG: Tinea).

options.text: Oral griseofulvin for 6 weeks given the multiple lesions and contact sport
options.rationale: Oral antifungals are for extensive disease, scalp/hair (tinea capitis), nails, or topical failure; not for limited tinea corporis. Would be correct with extensive or scalp involvement (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Topical hydrocortisone 1% twice daily for the inflammatory component plus oral antihistamines
options.rationale: Topical steroid on tinea (tinea incognito) reduces inflammation transiently but worsens fungal growth and atypical spread; antihistamines do not treat infection. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and exclusion from sport for presumed superficial bacterial infection
options.rationale: Microscopy confirms fungal cause; antibacterial therapy inappropriate. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Tinea corporis is a dermatophyte infection presenting as annular scaly plaques with active scaly raised borders and central clearing, common in contact sports through shared mats and equipment. First-line is topical terbinafine 1% twice daily for 1 to 2 weeks, plus equipment hygiene and treatment of affected teammates (eTG: Tinea).
key_points: Direct microscopy of skin scrapings positive for septate hyphae confirms dermatophyte (eTG: Tinea).
Topical terbinafine, miconazole, or clotrimazole BD x 2 weeks for limited disease (eTG: Tinea).
Oral antifungal for extensive disease, scalp, nails, or topical failure (eTG: Tinea).
Contact sport: do not return until lesions are no longer scaly and cannot be covered if competing (Sports Medicine Australia).
explanation.pearls: Pityriasis rosea looks similar but is scrape-negative and follows a Christmas-tree distribution along Langer's lines - and the herald patch precedes the eruption by 1 to 2 weeks, not 10 days.

subtopic_detail: Erythema multiforme vs Stevens-Johnson syndrome - recognition and triage
stem: An 11-year-old boy presents to a paediatric emergency department with a rash and oral pain. He started oral lamotrigine 3 weeks ago after a recent diagnosis of focal epilepsy with secondary generalisation, and the dose was rapidly titrated by his treating neurologist alongside ongoing sodium valproate, which he had been taking for 6 months. He has had no other new medications, no recent herpes simplex outbreak, and no recent Mycoplasma-like respiratory illness. Over the last 36 hours he has developed a flu-like prodrome with fever to 38.7 deg C, malaise, photophobia, and a sore mouth and eyes. Today the rash has spread rapidly. On examination he is unwell, mildly tachycardic, with temperature 38.6 deg C, heart rate 124, respiratory rate 22, SpO2 98% room air, and BP 105/65. There are confluent dusky erythematous macules with central duskiness and bullae forming on the trunk and proximal limbs covering an estimated 8% body surface area, with sloughing on light lateral pressure on intact-appearing skin. He has haemorrhagic crusting of the lips, painful ulcers on the buccal mucosa and palate, bilateral red painful eyes with conjunctival injection and mucopurulent discharge, and dysuria with genital erosions visible on inspection. There are some flat lesions on the palms but no classic three-zone targets. He is alert, distressed, and able to drink only sips.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6 deg C, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 98% RA, BP 105/65
data_table.Examination: Dusky macules with central blistering on 8% BSA, positive lateral skin shear (Nikolsky), mucous membrane involvement of mouth, eyes, and genitals
data_table.Medications: Lamotrigine started 3 weeks ago, rapidly titrated

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Switch lamotrigine to oral carbamazepine and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
options.rationale: SSSS affects younger children (under 5), spares mucosae, no target lesions or drug history; carbamazepine is also an aromatic anticonvulsant that cross-reacts with lamotrigine in SCAR. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Continue lamotrigine at half dose and start oral prednisolone for presumed erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: EM has three-zone target lesions, acral, usually post-HSV; this case is dusky macules with sloughing and positive Nikolsky pointing to SJS. Half-dose lamotrigine continues the offending drug. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical IV aciclovir and admit to a general paediatric ward for HSV-associated erythema multiforme major
options.rationale: HSV-associated EM has classic targets and minimal-to-modest mucosal involvement; diffuse dusky macules with bullae and Nikolsky in a child on a high-risk drug fits SJS. Would be correct only in HSV-associated EM (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Cease lamotrigine immediately, admit to a paediatric burns/HDU service, and consult ophthalmology and dermatology urgently
options.rationale: Bullae plus positive Nikolsky sign plus involvement of at least two mucosal sites plus recent high-risk drug (lamotrigine, particularly with rapid titration or with concurrent valproate) is Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN spectrum. Immediate cessation of the offending agent, admission to a paediatric burns or HDU equivalent for fluid and skin care, and urgent ophthalmology review (ocular sequelae are the leading long-term morbidity) are mandatory (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome; RCH CPG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol, and arrange neurology review in 2 weeks for lamotrigine dose review
options.rationale: SJS has 5-15 percent mortality with delayed care and sight-threatening ocular sequelae; outpatient management is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN is a drug-induced, life-threatening mucocutaneous reaction with epidermal necrosis. Lamotrigine (especially with rapid titration or concurrent valproate) is one of the highest-risk agents. Stop the drug immediately, admit to burns/HDU, and engage ophthalmology early to prevent permanent ocular morbidity (eTG: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
key_points: SJS: <10% BSA detachment; TEN: >30%; SJS/TEN overlap 10 to 30% (eTG: SJS).
Highest-risk drugs in children: lamotrigine, allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin), NSAIDs (eTG: SJS).
Mucosal involvement >=2 sites is required for SJS - eye involvement is the leading long-term morbidity driver (eTG: SJS).
Add to ADR / Lifetime Drug Alert / Australian Vaccine and Medicine Allergy Service notification (TGA).
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine titration too fast (or alongside valproate, which doubles the lamotrigine level) is the classic exam trigger - in real life, titration must follow product information to halve the SJS risk.

subtopic_detail: Atypical exanthem with shock - differentiating toxin-mediated rashes
stem: A 6-year-old previously well fully immunised girl is brought to a tertiary paediatric emergency department by her parents. Five days ago she had varicella that was treated supportively at home, with the rash now beginning to crust over and no new lesions for 48 hours. Three days ago she developed pain in her left thigh which has been progressively worsening - she now refuses to bear weight on that leg and her parents describe the pain as seeming out of proportion to the modest external skin changes they can see. Over the last 12 hours she has become febrile to 39.8 deg C, vomited twice without diarrhoea, and developed a diffuse blanching macular erythematous rash particularly on the trunk and the affected thigh. On arrival she is grey, mottled, and confused, with temperature 39.6 deg C, heart rate 178, respiratory rate 48, SpO2 95% on room air, BP 70/35, and capillary refill 6 seconds in the limbs and 5 seconds centrally. Her left thigh has a 6 x 8 cm area of dusky erythema overlying a healing varicella lesion, with marked tenderness extending well beyond the visible erythema and a small area of skin anaesthesia centrally. There is no obvious crepitus on the limb. Bloods show WCC 24 x 10^9/L with marked left shift, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L (baseline normal), ALT 240 U/L, sodium 129 mmol/L, and platelets 92 x 10^9/L.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 deg C, HR 178, RR 48, SpO2 95% RA, BP 70/35, CRT 6 sec
data_table.Examination: Diffuse blanching erythroderma, dusky tender 6 x 8 cm patch on thigh overlying healing varicella, pain extending beyond erythema, focal skin anaesthesia
data_table.Investigations: WCC 24, CRP 285 mg/L, lactate 5.6, creatinine 110, ALT 240

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: MRI of the left thigh before intervention to delineate the extent of soft tissue involvement
options.rationale: Imaging must NOT delay surgical exploration in suspected nec fasc; diagnosis is surgical and operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Would never be correct (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Admit, commence IVIG 2 g/kg over 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin for atypical Kawasaki disease
options.rationale: Atypical KD can mimic but pain disproportionate to skin findings, lactic acidosis, AKI, dusky soft tissue over healing varicella point to invasive bacterial supra-infection. Would be correct only with KD-specific features (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Empirical IV aciclovir monotherapy for disseminated varicella with secondary haemodynamic compromise and septic shock
options.rationale: Disseminated varicella in immunocompetent child is rare; focal painful thigh with anaesthesia and toxin-mediated picture point to bacterial supra-infection. Would be correct only with disseminated VZV (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral phenoxymethylpenicillin and admit to a general paediatric ward for scarlet fever observation
options.rationale: Scarlet fever has sandpaper rash with strawberry tongue, not shock/lactic acidosis/AKI/pain disproportionate to skin. Would be correct in scarlet fever pattern (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Resuscitate, give IV ceftriaxone, vancomycin and clindamycin, and call surgery for urgent debridement
options.rationale: Post-varicella, pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, shock and multi-organ derangement (acute kidney injury, transaminitis, lactic acidosis) is invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection plus streptococcal toxic shock syndrome. Broad-spectrum cover (e.g. ceftriaxone) PLUS anti-staphylococcal (vancomycin) PLUS clindamycin (toxin suppression and ribosomal action) are essential, with urgent surgical debridement the definitive intervention - antibiotics do not penetrate necrotic tissue (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection; RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).

explanation.summary: Post-varicella invasive group A streptococcal infection with necrotising soft tissue infection and streptococcal toxic shock syndrome is a paediatric emergency. Recognition rests on pain out of proportion to skin findings, focal anaesthesia, dusky skin, erythroderma, and rapid multi-organ failure. Resuscitate, broad antibiotics including clindamycin, and urgent surgical debridement (eTG: Necrotising soft tissue infection).
key_points: Pain out of proportion + focal anaesthesia + dusky skin + shock = nec fasc until proven otherwise (eTG).
Clindamycin is critical - inhibits exotoxin production (eTG).
Definitive treatment is surgical - imaging must not delay theatre (RCH CPG: Necrotising fasciitis).
iGAS post-varicella is a recognised pattern in Australian and international cohorts (CDNA: iGAS SoNG).
explanation.pearls: If you are not sure whether to call the surgeons, you already are - operative inspection is both diagnostic and therapeutic and there is no safer time than now.
